title: approvals — model relationships
description: Seven owned entities. `ApprovableAction` is a platform-level registry (no tenant scope). `ApprovalTemplate` is tenant-scoped + application-scoped + versioned. `ApprovalTemplateApprover` normalises the approver-group set per template (design.md D-A7 rejected the single-JSON-column shortcut). `ApprovalInstance` is one row per triggered flow; `ApprovalRequirement` is one row per approver-group per instance; `ApprovalDecision` is one row per approver's decision. `ApprovalReminder` audits every reminder-dispatch to avoid duplicate sends. Outbound relations reach tenants, applications, users, and the polymorphic subject (Invoice / AccessGrant / Athlete / etc).

internal.description: Multiple tenants author multiple templates targeting the same approvable action. RESTRICT on delete of ApprovableAction while any template references it.
Every template targets exactly one approvable action. FK is by natural key (`action_key`) not surrogate id — this lets the seeder upsert action rows without breaking existing templates.
Approver groups are owned by the template. Cascade on template hard-delete (retention-purge only).
Instances reference the template that spawned them. RESTRICT on delete — retention-purge requires all instances to be purged first.
The template snapshot at instance-create time. `template_version` column on the instance row pins the semantic version — subsequent template edits do not retroactively change existing instances.
Requirements are materialised at instance-create time. Cascade on instance hard-delete (retention-purge). Ordered by sort_order ASC.
The template approver group the requirement was materialised from. RESTRICT so a group edit doesn't corrupt in-flight instances.
Decisions attach to a requirement. Cascade on requirement hard-delete (only via instance cascade).

outbound.description: Tenant-scoped. Every template belongs to exactly one tenant.
Application-scoped. A Sports refund template doesn't apply to Marketplace. RESTRICT so application-disable doesn't corrupt tenant governance rules.
Denormalised from parent template's tenant_id for query-plan + FK integrity.
The tenant User who triggered the action. RESTRICT so an approval's audit trail can't be orphaned by a user-hard-delete (compliance retention outlives users).
The approver whose decision tipped the quorum. Null while status='pending'. Set exactly once at transition to approved/rejected. RESTRICT for the same audit-integrity reason as `requester_id`.

The resource the action operates on. Polymorphic — could be Invoice, AccessGrant, Athlete, RoleAssignment, ImpersonationSession, etc. Nullable when the action doesn't have a well-defined subject (e.g. bulk-operation approvals). The IsApprovable trait on the subject-side provides the inverse `->approvals()` relation.
The User who made the decision. RESTRICT for audit-integrity.
Set when the approver was acting under an active `access/delegation::RoleDelegation`. Null when the approver was the resolved user directly (no delegation involved). RESTRICT for audit-integrity.
The reminded approver. Cascade on user-hard-delete — reminder rows have no independent audit value beyond the underlying requirement + decision.

inbound.description: Two categories of inbound relations. (1) The IsApprovable marker trait on subject models (Invoice, AccessGrant, Athlete, RoleAssignment, ImpersonationSession, ...) provides a polymorphic inverse `->approvals()` to their referencing `approval_instances` rows — subject models are NOT stored with a hard FK; the relation is polymorphic via `subject_type + subject_id`. (2) The HasApprovalHistory trait on the `User` model exposes `->approvalsRequested()` / `->approvalsPendingReview()` / `->approvalsDecided()` relations. Neither category adds any FK to this module's tables — the relations are read-side only.

cross_module_write_paths.description: The engine WRITES rows in this module via the middleware + observer chain; it does NOT reach into other modules' tables. Cross-module state transitions happen exclusively via events + listener bridges — see events.json + listeners.json for the full flow.
flow: action decorated with #[AsApprovableAction] is invoked → approve middleware intercepts → ApprovalInstanceProvisioner writes approval_instances + approval_requirements rows
approver POSTs approve → ApprovalDecisionRecorder writes approval_decisions row → ApprovalRequirementObserver recomputes quorum → transitions requirement + instance

quorum satisfied on all requirements → ApprovalGranted event fires → ApprovalInstanceExecutor dispatches the underlying action with `scoped('approvals.executing')=$instance_id` flag set → approve middleware sees the flag and passes through
action completes → ApprovalInstanceExecutor updates approval_instances.status='executed' + executed_at → ApprovalExecuted event fires

cross-module bridges listen for ApprovalExecuted with matching action_key (e.g. AccessRequestGrantMaterializer for access.grants.create) → they call the DOWNSTREAM module's public API (e.g. access/grants::GrantIssuer::issue()) — never a direct DB write
